replies.author: @vipe650r
replies.text:
@NeutronStarU I agree that not knowing how or when to evolve your characters and the stories you tell absolutely results in a franchise going stale. Marvel has needed to and failed to evolve with direction since Endgame, for the most part (with a few bright spots along the way).

I would strongly disagree that different is always better. Sometimes different is a TERRIBLE idea. Wisdom is knowing WHAT not to change, as well as what TO change. Maturity is treating your audience like people with brains and fleshing out the journey there so that we join you for the trip. Change for change's sake is foolishness. There needs to be a reason behind it, and a quality to it. 

If it was fun for you, I don't share that, but go for it. It's totally fine to want different things from a movie. Some movies I go to because I don't want to think. I want dumb jokes, pretty pictures, and a silly time. And I walk out content. 

Love and Thunder - specifically with Ragnarok righly held up as a framework - was certainly allowed to be different, and needed to be unique and its own movie. We didn't need a Ragnarok 2, I will definitly agree with that. 

For my part, even the actors and director (sort of) acknowledge problems it had. Bale should not have even been in the movie. Since they were never going to utilize him properly, some Marvel villain-of-the-week as a plot-driver for a tighter story would have been better. That would have possibly allowed the deeply moving Cancer/Jane Foster Thor to actually be fleshed out and sat with. And Waititi's ability to blend a bit of drama with humor, without undercutting either one (something Marvel is often accused of). Unfortunately, we didn't get that... I'm not sure we got much of anything.

replies.text:
⁠@Galgus2000Yeah. I rewatched it recently for the first time after seeing it in the theater and not really liking it. On the rewatch, I noticed the movie has a lot more heart than people give it credit for. The whole Jane/cancer subplot is actually handled really well, and it brings out the best performances in the movie from Chris Hemsworth and Natalie Portman. Gorr’s story is quite compelling, too, and the ending with his daughter is also kind of touching. 

On a smaller screen, the visuals actually hold up (or at least better than they did on a big movie screen). Yeah, some CGI work is shoddy, but I chalk that up to the movie being made during the pandemic. Some of the imagery is actually kind of striking, like the scene done mostly in black and white. 

Overall, I think Love and Thunder needs a reappraisal. It got a lot of hate for what it wasn’t- a faithful adaptation of the God Butcher comic- but that’s not quite fair. Judge something for what is is, not what it isn’t.

@lisaleone2296 I mean I don't think it's fair to say nobody wants female action heroes - the problem is that the current archetype that Hollywood keeps writing is a chick who is completely flawless, more powerful and intelligent than every man around her, and unapologetically smug about it. When the script gives the hero no character arc (because she doesn't need to grow), no competition (because everyone else just sucks in comparison) and no humility, that's not someone we want to root for. I think if you look at any GOOD story that has action elements with a balanced cast of characters, you'll find a good female action hero - they just don't go out of their way to do the girl-boss freeze frame so that you tag it in your mind that way. 

Like, say we focus on Star Wars shows - yeah, there's a whole bunch of shitty examples (the Acoloyte, as the obvious one, but Asohka and Kenobi had a lot of dumb stuff too), but think about all the female characters doing cool action shit there were in Andor! They just didn't go out of their way to advertise the *GIRL POWER* of it all.

author: @thecornerkid402
text: I’m reminded of an interview Jack White gave years ago. He said that he intentionally gives himself roadblocks when he’s working. He’ll have a list of instruments he can use and add no more. He’ll limit the colors that can be on the album. Schedules a limited amount of studio time. Give himself a deadline that he has to meet. Place his instruments in inconvenient places on stage, have the guitars be out of tune when he enters so he has to work at something during his performances. He said the reason is that unlimited resources are the enemy of creativity. When you’re in a strict box you have to come up with creative solutions so he makes sure that there’s a box around him at all times.

replies.text: This EXACTLY what's happened to videogames. The limitations of the hardware meant people had to get creative when making their games. Outrun is one example i like to use. Because of the hardware Yu Suzuki had to find workarounds to try and implement driving fast with the limits of the board at the time. And because of that Outrun remains a classic to this day. Modern PCs etc have so much power now you can do anything you like, but since so creativity went out the window.

He didn't have resources when he made that movie, but he knew he had an interesting story, that could be way bigger, if he used the editing of the movie cleverly. replies.author: @gnarwhal7562
replies.text: Well put. Another great example of this idea is the Ren & Stimpy Show. The animators had to find creative ways to sneak certain jokes and topics past the censors, which led to memorable gags like "Happy Happy Joy Joy" and "These Hands...". Then in the 2000s, Spike TV greenlit Ren & Stimpy Adult Party Cartoon, which gave them unlimited freedom with mature topics. This actually ended up working to the show's disadvantage though, as without the censors restricting them, the animators devolved into cheap shock humor and innuendos for plotlines, and the whole thing became an ugly mess in comparison to the original series. People are always at their most creative when limited in some way

replies.author: @BrandyGunderson
replies.text:
@wbae1340. A movie can be both a good movie and a bad use of that specific character/setting/mythos, I.e. a bad movie for that specific character.   Kinda like what Marvel has been doing recently with everything, trying to maintain the same comedic voice in every film for every character.   But Captain America is not Spider-Man.  Nor is Hawkeye, Black Widow, Captain Marvel, Shang-chi, Hulk, Daredevil, etc.  So having them quip like Spidey makes for a bad Captain America/Hawkeye/Black Widow/etc.  movie.  Thor isn’t a sitcom character.  He’s operatic.  The guy has literally been fighting the forces of chaos to protect Asgard and Midgard for millennia. He regularly deals with matters of cosmic importance.  So having every member of the cast constantly making jokes, having running gags, etc. makes for a poor Thor movie.

Now, you can make a really good sitcom movie that has a guy named Thor in it.  Just like Nolan made a really good crime drama movie with a guy named Batman in it.  But they aren’t the same things as Thor or Batman movies.

author: @dragonstryk7280
text:
When Peter Jackson went to make Lord of the Rings, he was very active in talking about how important it was that he get the details right, because, as he stated, the LotR fans would NEVER let him hear the end of it if he screwed it up.

There's a humility in that statement that forces the creator to keep themselves in bounds. For the biggest names, those boundaries more or less disappear, and it takes a rare mind to be able to handle unrestricted power. We're taught to believe that artists need to be unrestricted, but the opposite is more often the case. "Oh but you can make anything you want" *sounds* good, but in practice, leads to choice paralysis. It's like when you go into a new place to eat, and someone tells you that you can get whatever you want. That's fine at Five Guys, where the menu choices are boiled down to what kind of burger and shake you want with your gallon of fries, but in places with much larger menus, you get people stuck cause they have no idea what to get at the crucial point. We've all seen that guy that's just staring blankly at a menu board like it's written in heiroglyphics cause there's too many options.

replies.text: The problem of the first Thor movies is that they’re boring. Thor in the comics is serious, yes, but he produces a lot of humor by juxtaposing his attitude with that of other characters (playing the straight man) and with his antiquated way of speaking. Asgard is a colorful, mythical and breathtaking place. In these movies, it looks like a generic sci-fi city with a population directly transferred from a modern American metropolis. Not to mention that the first movie barely even shows Asgard, and the second one butchered Malekith beyond recognition. In the end, Thor 1 and Thor Dark World are bar Thor movies, Ragnarok and Love and Thunder aren't Thor movies to begin with.
